commands.review_summary:
This PR changes shared hostname normalization to strip repeated trailing dots and adds regression coverage across the normalizer, SSRF policy checks, trusted env proxy dispatch, and SSRF test helper parity.

PR surface: Source 0, Tests +41. Total +41 across 5 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection plus a Node URL check shows current main preserves repeated-dot hostnames while normalizeHostname strips only one dot before the reserved-host policy checks; I did not run Vitest in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: Manual review is required because this protected, security-sensitive PR changes shared SSRF behavior and lacks after-fix real behavior proof; no automated code repair is identified.

target.title: Harden hostname normalization for repeated trailing dots [AI]
target.body:
## Summary

- Harden hostname canonicalization so repeated terminal dots are treated the same as a canonical hostname before network guard policy checks.
- Add regression coverage for blocked hostnames with repeated trailing dots at the normalizer, SSRF policy, and trusted environment proxy dispatch boundary.
- Keep SSRF test helpers on the shared production hostname normalizer to avoid drift in plugin-facing test utilities.
- Keep the change scoped to shared hostname normalization and network guard tests; no config, migration, or public API changes are intended.
- AI-assisted.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

No public issue is closed by this PR body.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

No public related context is linked here.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No public maintainer request is linked here.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Reserved hostnames with repeated trailing dots are blocked before trusted proxy dispatch.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on Node/Vitest via the repository test wrapper.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/net/hostname.test.ts src/infra/net/ssrf.test.ts src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ssrf.test.ts extensions/google/image-generation-provider.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: The focused wrapper reported 4 test files passed and 158 tests passed.
- Observed result after fix: Repeated-dot variants of `localhost`, `metadata.google.internal`, `.localhost`, `.local`, and `.internal` hostnames are rejected before DNS lookup or fetch dispatch in trusted proxy mode.
- What was not tested: Live proxy forwarding against a real external proxy or cloud metadata service.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The coverage uses mocked fetch/proxy/runtime dependencies to prove the guard decision without performing external network calls.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Existing normalization stripped only one trailing dot, leaving repeated-dot hostnames non-canonical for exact and suffix checks.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/net/hostname.test.ts src/infra/net/ssrf.test.ts src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ssrf.test.ts`

`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/net/hostname.test.ts src/infra/net/ssrf.test.ts src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ssrf.test.ts extensions/google/image-generation-provider.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Canonical hostname normalization now covers repeated trailing dots. SSRF policy tests cover blocked reserved hostnames with repeated trailing dots. Trusted proxy guard tests cover rejection before lookup or fetch dispatch. The plugin-facing SSRF test helper now reuses the shared normalizer.

What failed before this fix, if known?

Repeated terminal dots could leave a hostname outside the canonical exact/suffix blocked forms.

If no test was added, why not?

Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, malformed or unusual repeated-dot forms of blocked hostnames are now rejected consistently.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, network hostname guard canonicalization is stricter for repeated trailing dots.

What is the highest-risk area?

Network guard hostname canonicalization is shared by SSRF policy and SDK-exposed helpers.

How is that risk mitigated?

The implementation is a narrow canonicalization change with focused normalizer, SSRF policy, trusted proxy guard, and helper-parity regression coverage.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Await automated checks and maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI and maintainer review are pending.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

None yet.
